blocks.title: Who this pathway covers
blocks.content: Adults presenting in primary care with dizziness, vertigo, imbalance, or light-headedness.

blocks.title: Core principle
blocks.content: Exclude stroke/central causes first. If red flags are absent and findings are peripheral/positional, manage in primary care first.

blocks.title: Immediate same-day emergency referral
items: Sudden-onset dizziness/vertigo with focal neurological deficit.
Acute vestibular syndrome not explained by BPPV/postural hypotension and no trained HINTS assessor available.
Concerning central features (e.g., central-pattern nystagmus, new severe gait unsteadiness, acute new deafness with vestibular syndrome).
Associated major neurological symptoms (diplopia, dysarthria/speech change, unilateral weakness/numbness, reduced consciousness, severe new headache).

blocks.title: Primary care assessment essentials
items: Classify dizziness phenotype (vertigo vs presyncope vs disequilibrium).
Establish onset, duration, triggers, recurrence pattern.
Perform focused neurological and gait examination.
Perform Hallpike where indicated and trained.

blocks.title: Primary care management
items: Use vestibular suppressants short-term only in acute severe phase.
Provide safety advice and encourage graded mobilisation.
Arrange reassessment if not improving or diagnosis remains uncertain.

blocks.title: Routine referral thresholds
items: BPPV persisting after appropriate repositioning manoeuvres.
Persistent dizziness/vertigo beyond ~6 weeks despite first-line care.
Recurrent suspected vestibular migraine/Meniere-type episodes not controlled in primary care.

blocks.title: Evidence confidence
blocks.content: Moderate-High. Strong NICE NG127 threshold evidence plus concordant Scottish board pathways; confidence reduced by CKS extraction limits and no dedicated current SIGN dizziness guideline identified in this run.
